page: Vultr vc2-2c-4gb is a Cloud Compute virtual machine featuring an x86_64 architecture powered by an Intel Xeon processor. It is configured with 2 shared vCPUs derived from 1 physical core with hyper-threading enabled, running at a clock speed of 2.0 GHz. The hardware allocation includes 4.0 GB of system memory, providing 2.0 GB of RAM per core, alongside 80 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. The instance also includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Benchmark results indicate entry-level performance characteristics. The shared CPU configuration yields a stress-ng single-core score of 1226.09 and a multi-core score of 2501.8, both placing in the bottom 25% of tested servers. Memory performance is similarly constrained, with large-block read bandwidth at 19.26 GB/sec and write bandwidth at 16.87 GB/sec. Database and transactional capabilities are modest, as shown by a Passmark database score of 374.96 and a Redis SET throughput of 1.36 million operations per second.

From a resource density and cost-efficiency perspective, this instance offers a low-cost entry point for workloads that do not require dedicated CPU resources or high memory bandwidth. The shared CPU allocation introduces potential performance variability, making it less suitable for sustained high-throughput tasks. It is best suited for lightweight, non-critical workloads such as basic web serving, development environments, small utility scripts, or low-traffic database testing.
description: Vultr vc2-2c-4gb is an entry-level Cloud Compute virtual machine featuring 2 shared vCPUs on an Intel Xeon x86_64 processor running at 2.0 GHz, paired with 4.0 GB of RAM and 80 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. Designed for budget-conscious deployments, this instance utilizes a shared CPU model that introduces resource-sharing tradeoffs, as reflected in its modest benchmark results. It achieves a stress-ng single-core score of 1226.09 and a multi-core score of 2501.8, placing it in the bottom 25% of performance tiers. Memory bandwidth and database operations also rank in the lower percentiles, with Redis SET operations reaching 1.36 million operations per second. Given these performance constraints, the instance is highly cost-efficient for lightweight workloads that do not require sustained compute power, such as basic web serving, development environments, and small utility tasks.
